our_argument_summary: WIN-011 is verdicted 'Not Demonstrated'. The dome claims a −6.5 μGal gravity anomaly during the 1997 Mohe eclipse confirms electromagnetic-gravitational coupling. Our review shows: (1) Wang et al. (2000) reported anomalies at first/last CONTACT, not eclipse maximum, and explicitly stated 'no significant anomaly during the very solar eclipse was found'; (2) Yang & Wang (2002) reanalysis weakened the evidence further; (3) Van Camp et al. (2001) found no signal with four superconducting gravimeters ~1000× more precise; (4) the coupling constant κ = 10.9/6.5 = 1.677 nT/μGal is a ratio of two hardcoded values validated against itself in monitor.py Domain #7.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Domain #7 in monitor.py computes kappa_obs = 10.9 / 6.5 = 1.677 nT/μGal, then compares to hardcoded KAPPA = 1.67 with 5% tolerance. The 10.9 nT is from WIN-010 (BOU 2017 eclipse magnetic anomaly), and 6.5 μGal is from WIN-011 (Mohe 1997). Both numerator and denominator are hardcoded literals — no gravimeter or magnetometer data is fetched.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely hardcoded. KAPPA = 1.67 is a constant. The 'observed' ratio 10.9/6.5 uses two hardcoded numbers. The 5% tolerance check passes trivially because predicted and observed are derived from the same source values. No external API or data source is queried.
data_sources_used: None — all values are hardcoded literals
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: 5% tolerance on coupling constant comparison — irrelevant since both sides are hardcoded from the same source.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review correctly identifies the circularity of the Domain #7 computation. Note: monitor.py is not available as a separate file in raw-text/ but the code behavior is well-documented from Cycle 1 analysis and referenced in multiple WIN entries.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Domain #7 in monitor.py uses hardcoded values: magnetic_anomaly=10.9, gravity_anomaly=6.5, KAPPA=1.67. The 'validation' computes 10.9/6.5=1.677 and checks abs(1.677-1.67)/1.67 < 0.05. No gravimeter data is fetched; no API call is made. All values are baked into the source code.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics does NOT predict an eclipse gravity anomaly. The Allais effect has been investigated for 60+ years and remains unconfirmed — SG instruments (Van Camp et al. 2001) found no signal. The dome is claiming a phenomenon that standard physics rejects or considers unconfirmed, not relabeling an existing standard explanation. This tag was incorrectly set to 'true' in Cycle 1 and in the current wins.json — it should be 'false'.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The 1997 Mohe eclipse observation was published by Wang et al. in 2000. The dome model was created circa 2024. The -6.5 μGal value was adopted from existing literature, not predicted in advance.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The -6.5 μGal value is taken directly from Wang et al. (2000). No dome parameter (disc_radius, H(r), aetheric index) is used to derive this number. The dome simply adopts the published observation as a 'confirmed prediction.'

holes_found.description: code_analysis tag 'relabels_standard' is incorrectly set to 'true' in wins.json. Standard physics does NOT predict an eclipse gravity anomaly — the phenomenon is unconfirmed/rejected by the scientific community. WIN-011 claims something standard physics does NOT explain, unlike WINs that rename ionospheric physics as 'aetheric.' WIN-013 and WIN-014 (the SG nulls) already correctly have relabels_standard=false, which is inconsistent with WIN-011 having relabels_standard=true. If standard physics predicts no eclipse gravity anomaly, a WIN claiming such an anomaly cannot be 'relabeling standard physics.'
holes_found.recommendation: Change code_analysis.relabels_standard from true to false in wins.json for WIN-011. This will affect Part 4.6 computed counts.

holes_found.description: detail_extra contains a GRACE L1A sentence that seems misplaced: 'The GRACE L1A claim conflates atmospheric drag with gravitational signal.' GRACE (launched 2002) measures satellite-to-satellite range changes to map Earth's gravity field — it is not related to eclipse gravimetry at Mohe. The GRACE L1A issue is more relevant to WIN-012's broader coupling constant argument or to a general discussion of gravity measurement methodology. In WIN-011's context, this sentence is a non sequitur that could confuse readers about what WIN-011 actually claims.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove or relocate the GRACE L1A sentence from WIN-011's detail_extra. If GRACE L1A is relevant to the WIN-011/012 group, place it in WIN-012's detail_extra instead, where it can be properly contextualized.

holes_found.description: The Van Camp et al. (2001) DOI (10.1023/A:1013940928637) uses Springer's old DOI format. This is technically valid but might not resolve for all users. The paper may also be cited as 'Prospective Seismological and Gravimetric Studies of the 1999 European Total Solar Eclipse' in the Bulletin d'Information des Marées Terrestres, or in a Journal of the Geodetic Society of Japan publication. Verifying DOI resolution would strengthen citation integrity.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify that DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 currently resolves to the correct Van Camp et al. paper about the 1999 eclipse SG measurements.

holes_found.description: Our review says Van Camp et al. used SGs 'roughly 1,000× more precise than the LaCoste-Romberg.' The precision ratio is approximately correct: LaCoste-Romberg spring gravimeters have a noise floor of ~1 μGal, while superconducting gravimeters achieve ~0.001 μGal (1 nGal). However, 'precision' and 'sensitivity' are different concepts, and a dome defender could argue that precision in laboratory conditions doesn't translate to field measurement accuracy during an eclipse. The phrasing is defensible but could be tightened.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding 'in terms of noise floor' after '1,000× more precise' to make the comparison more specific.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's treatment of WIN-011 vs WIN-013/WIN-014 creates a logical trap: WIN-011 claims the Mohe gravimeter detected a real gravity anomaly (−6.5 μGal), while WIN-013 and WIN-014 claim that SG instruments detecting ZERO gravity anomaly also confirms the model (because the coupling is 'electromagnetic, not gravitational'). This means the dome claims credit for both positive AND null detections — a textbook unfalsifiable construction. If ANY gravimeter result confirms the model, the model cannot be tested by gravimetry. Our review touches on this in WIN-013/014 but could make the logical connection more explicit in WIN-011 by noting that the dome's own subsequent WINs effectively disavow the very measurement WIN-011 rests on.
sources: WIN-013 verdict: Misleading — 'claiming credit for opposite outcomes makes model unfalsifiable'
WIN-014 verdict: Misleading — 'Same logical contradiction as WIN-013'

stronger_arguments.description: The Wang et al. (2000) paper's actual conclusion is devastating for the dome's claim. The abstract states the anomalies appeared at 'first contact and fourth contact' — the moments of minimum eclipse coverage — not at totality where gravitational shielding (if real) would peak. This timing signature is inconsistent with ANY shielding or coupling mechanism. The dome ignores the timing and cherry-picks only the magnitude (−6.5 μGal). Our review already makes this point but could emphasize that the timing signature alone rules out gravitational shielding regardless of the magnitude question.
sources: Wang et al. 2000, Phys. Rev. D 62, 041101(R)

kernel_of_truth.description: Eclipse gravity experiments are legitimate scientific investigations. The Wang et al. paper was published in Physical Review D, a top-tier physics journal. The Mohe measurement was conducted with standard protocols and a calibrated instrument. The dome model correctly identifies that the eclipse gravity question remains scientifically open — mainstream physics has not definitively ruled out all possible eclipse-gravity effects, only shown that any such effect is below the SG detection threshold (~0.1 nGal).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being scientifically open is not the same as being confirmed. The dome model treats an unconfirmed, contested measurement as established fact, ignores that superior instruments found nothing, misrepresents the timing of the anomaly, and uses this single data point to derive a 'fundamental coupling constant.' Furthermore, the dome's own WIN-013/014 (SG nulls) logically undermine the very measurement WIN-011 depends on — if the coupling is electromagnetic and not gravitational, then a gravimeter cannot detect it, and the Mohe reading must be noise.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 review of WIN-011 finds that the Cycle 1 major issues (misquoted +15.7 μGal and incorrect Tibet location) have been corrected — the detail_claim now accurately reflects −6.5 μGal at Mohe, Heilongjiang Province. The detail_evidence is substantially improved with proper attribution to Wang et al. (2000), the timing distinction (contacts vs. maximum), Van Camp et al. (2001) SG null result, and the coupling constant circularity.

New finding for Cycle 2: The code_analysis tag 'relabels_standard' is incorrectly set to 'true' in wins.json. This is a significant error because standard physics does NOT predict eclipse gravity anomalies — it effectively rejects them (SG instruments found nothing). WIN-011 claims a phenomenon standard physics does NOT explain, which is the opposite of relabeling. The current tag incorrectly inflates the 'relabels standard physics' count in Part 4.6. WIN-013 and WIN-014 already correctly have relabels_standard=false, making WIN-011's 'true' tag internally inconsistent within the eclipse-gravity cluster.

Additional finding: The detail_extra contains a GRACE L1A sentence that is a non sequitur in the WIN-011 context. GRACE measures satellite gravity fields and is unrelated to ground-based eclipse gravimetry. This sentence likely belongs in WIN-012 or should be removed.

The verdict 'Not Demonstrated' remains appropriate. The claim rests entirely on an unconfirmed spring-gravimeter detection contradicted by four superconducting gravimeters, and the dome's own subsequent WINs (013/014) logically disavow the very measurement WIN-011 depends on.

citations_verified: Wang et al. 2000, Phys. Rev. D 62, 041101 — DOI format is correct for Physical Review D volume 62 (2000). Rapid Communication about Mohe 1997 eclipse gravity.
Yang & Wang 2002, Astrophys. Space Sci. 282, 245-253 — citation format appears correct for journal, volume, pages.
citations_unchecked: Van Camp et al. (2001) DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 — old Springer DOI format; should be verified for current resolution. The citation may reference either the BIM/Journal of Geodetic Society publication.
GRACE JPL link (https://grace.jpl.nasa.gov/) — link likely active but GRACE L1A context questionable for WIN-011
